preFix.title: Breathable insulation for pre-1919 solid-wall buildings
preFix.subtitle: Hemp, wood fibre, and lime plaster as alternatives to PIR for older homes
preFix.excerpt: Solid-wall homes built before 1919 use lime mortar and unpainted brick or stone that absorbs and releases moisture. Sealing them with PIR board and a vapour barrier traps moisture and causes accelerated decay. Breathable insulation materials let the wall continue to buffer moisture while cutting heat loss by 30-50%. This explains the difference and how to choose the right material.
preFix.sourceNotes: Historic England guidance 'Insulating Old Buildings' (2017). SPAB (Society for the Protection of Ancient Buildings) guidance on breathable materials. Centre for Alternative Technology (CAT) technical notes on hemp and wood fibre insulation. Lime Technology technical data sheets for hemp and lime insulation batts.

content.text: A Victorian terrace in Bristol was built with 220 mm solid brick walls laid in lime mortar. The brick and mortar together act as a moisture buffer: rain that penetrates the outer leaf is absorbed, redistributed, and slowly evaporated over weeks. This evaporation mechanism is what keeps the wall and the rooms on either side relatively dry. Install a polyethylene
vapour control layer
and
PIR board

against that wall, and the moisture that would normally evaporate has nowhere to go. It concentrates at the back of the insulation, soaking into the lime mortar joints and the back of the brick. In ten years, the mortar is soft and the brick face is spalling.
Which buildings need breathable insulation

Any building with solid walls and lime mortar joints. This covers nearly all UK housing built before 1919: Victorian terraces and semis, Edwardian detached houses, Georgian and earlier construction, and most rural stone cottages. The test: press a nail into a mortar joint. If the mortar is cream or buff coloured and soft enough for the nail to penetrate 5 mm without crumbling, it is lime mortar.
Listed buildings and conservation areas often restrict what materials can be used. Check with your local planning office before doing any internal wall insulation.

Post-1919 solid-brick houses with OPC (ordinary Portland cement) mortar are less sensitive to vapour barriers. The denser mortar and harder brick allow some use of PIR board with careful detailing, though breathable materials are still lower-risk.
The breathable material options
Hemp-lime batts

Hemp-lime batts combine hemp shiv (the woody core of hemp stalks) with a lime binder. Lambda value: 0.040-0.060 W/m·K. For a meaningful thermal improvement on a 220 mm brick wall (existing U-value roughly 2.0 W/m²·K), 100 mm of hemp-lime batts reduces the U-value to approximately 0.45 W/m²·K. The same result requires 40 mm of PIR, but hemp-lime is vapour-open: moisture passes through rather than accumulating.
Wood fibre board

Wood fibre insulation boards are pressed timber fibre bonded with natural resins. Lambda value: 0.038-0.042 W/m·K. Available in 40-200 mm thicknesses; rigid boards can be plastered directly or used as substrate for wet plaster render. More dimensionally stable than hemp-lime batts; suitable in bathrooms where humidity is high. 100 mm achieves roughly the same U-value as the hemp-lime example above.
Lime render with integrated insulation

Where wall depth is limited, a 20-30 mm lime render with perlite or hemp aggregate adds some thermal resistance (lambda ~0.12 W/m·K) while staying fully vapour-open. It will not reach a 0.3 W/m²·K target, but in a listed building where depth is unavailable it is better than bare lime plaster.
The installation sequence

Remove existing skirting, picture rails, and architraves. Inspect the wall surface for areas of failed lime plaster. Re-fix loose plaster sections with lime putty; allow two weeks to cure before insulating.

Fix wood fibre boards to the wall with lime mortar dabs. Do not use cement-based adhesive; it is too hard and rigid for an old wall that moves seasonally. Allow 25 mm overlap at board joints and stagger vertical joints between rows.

Apply a two-coat lime render over the boards. Scratch coat (NHL 3.5 natural hydraulic lime, 10 mm), allow three to seven days to cure, then finish coat (5 mm lime plaster). Total drying time before painting: four to six weeks.
Common variations

North-facing walls in exposed locations need more depth than south-facing walls in the same house. The north face sees more driving rain and more frost. Specify 120 mm of wood fibre on north and west faces; 80 mm is sufficient on south and east.

A stone cottage in Scotland or Wales using traditional rubble stone construction: the wall breathes differently than a brick wall, with much more moisture movement. Hemp-lime insulation with lime plaster is the lowest-risk choice. Avoid timber-framed linings in rubble stone walls. The timber creates a
thermal bridge
at each stud and tends to trap moisture at the wall face.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: the wall is noticeably warmer to the touch. Damp patches in cold corners clear over the first winter. The lime plaster is left unpainted for the first year so the wall can continue to breathe while the lime sets.

Done badly: PIR board bonded to a lime wall with a vapour barrier. Within two to three years, the lime mortar joints behind the board soften, the board delaminates from the wall, and the plaster surface cracks as the board moves.

attrs.fix: Hairline cracks in lime plaster are normal during curing. Brush with a dampened brush to close them, or apply a thin slurry coat of lime putty. Do not use flexible filler; it is a different material chemistry.
attrs.cause: The lime render has dried too fast, or the scratch coat was not allowed to cure before the finish coat was applied
attrs.symptom: New plaster surface shows hairline cracks within six months

attrs.fix: Leave a 5-10 mm air gap between the bottom of the insulation board and the floor slab before refitting skirting, or use a breathable foam backing behind the skirting.
attrs.cause: The skirting is blocking the evaporative path at the base of the wall; moisture is accumulating where air circulation is poor
attrs.symptom: Moisture tide mark appears above the skirting after the first winter

initialFindings.message: brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
